title: The Last Afternoon We Had
excerpt: There are places in the world that exist only in memory — golden, untouchable, already grieving themselves before you even leave.
content:
It was a Sunday, the kind that smells like cut grass and old wooden porches. My grandmother's house sat at the end of a road that curved like a question mark, and we always arrived already talking, already laughing before we had even parked the car.

That last afternoon, nothing announced itself as final. The table was the same. The dishes were the same. Even the crack in the ceiling above the dining room, shaped vaguely like a river, was exactly as I had always known it. And yet, somewhere between the second cup of tea and the moment my cousin started telling a story about her first day at work, I felt it — a softness pressing from the inside out, the kind that arrives just before you lose something.

My grandmother moved slowly around the kitchen. She had always moved slowly, but now it was different. Now it was deliberate, like she was learning the room again. I watched her hands wrap around a cup. Hands that had kneaded bread, braided hair, written letters no one had ever thrown away.

I do not know how to explain what it means to love a place that belongs to another person. But I know that house was never just hers. It was the smell of cardamom and old books. It was the sound the gate made when you pushed it too fast. It was afternoons that lasted longer than hours should allow.

We drove home as the sun dropped behind the trees. My mother was quiet in the passenger seat. I did not ask her what she was thinking. I already knew.

Some endings do not arrive with noise. They arrive like that Sunday — unhurried, ordinary, impossibly tender.

author.name: Leila Mansour
author.bio: Leila writes about memory, family, and the quiet architecture of belonging. Her essays have appeared in literary journals across the Middle East and Europe.

title: How Silence Taught Me to Listen
excerpt: A week without speaking changed the way I hear the world — the exhale of a city, the grammar of strangers, the sound of my own thinking.
content:
I did not go on a silent retreat to find myself. I went because I was exhausted from narrating my own life out loud. For years, every experience had come with a running commentary — sent to friends, posted in captions, recounted over dinner. Somewhere underneath all that language, I had lost the event itself.

The retreat was held in a stone building on the edge of a hill. There were twelve of us. On the first morning, the facilitator said simply: silence begins now. And it did.

The first day was the hardest. My mind compensated for the absence of speech with extraordinary noise. Every thought arrived louder than it should have. I catalogued anxieties. I rehearsed imaginary conversations. I made lists of things I needed to do the moment I could speak again.

By the third day, something loosened. I started to hear what had always been there. The particular pitch of wind through a gap in a door frame. The rhythm of other people eating — each person with their own tempo, their own rituals. My own breathing, which I had apparently forgotten to notice for most of my adult life.

There is a quality of attention that is only available in stillness. Not the forced stillness of waiting, but the chosen stillness of deciding that this moment, as it is, is enough to be inside of.

When I spoke again on the last morning, my voice sounded strange to me. Smaller, somehow, than I remembered. Which felt right. I had been taking up more space than I needed to.

I still talk too much. But now I notice when I do.

author.name: Omar Shaheen
author.bio: Omar is a writer and occasional wanderer based in Cairo. He is interested in the space between experience and the stories we tell about it.

title: Night Shift
excerpt: At 3 a.m., the city belongs to a different set of people — the ones the daylight crowd never thinks to ask about.
content:
The bakery opens at two in the morning. By the time most people are ordering their first coffee of the day, Karim has already been at work for six hours.

He does not complain about this. He learned the rhythm from his father, who learned it from his, who once ran a bread stall in a market that no longer exists in a street that has been renamed three times. The hours are strange, but the work is familiar in a way that makes him feel located.

I met Karim through a neighbor who mentioned, almost in passing, that the best bread in our district came from a small bakery that was impossible to find before sunrise. I went once as a kind of experiment. I kept going because of the bread, and then because of the conversation.

The night city is different. That is a cliché because it is true. The streets carry a different kind of human traffic after midnight — the nurses and the cleaners and the security guards and the bakers, the people who maintain the infrastructure of comfort that the daytime city takes entirely for granted.

Karim says he prefers it. The silence, the focus, the particular satisfaction of finishing before most people have started. There is also, he says, something honest about this shift. You cannot perform at 3 a.m. You are just yourself, doing the thing.

I walked home with a bag of bread as the sky turned the specific grey-pink that belongs only to cities on the edge of dawn. I had been awake all night. I felt, unexpectedly, completely awake.

author.name: Nadia Farouq
author.bio: Nadia writes long-form narrative journalism about the people and places that cities tend to overlook. She is based in Alexandria.

title: The Garden That Outlasted Everything
excerpt: My father planted a fig tree the year I was born. It is still there. He is not. Some things take root in ways that have nothing to do with soil.
content:
There is a photograph I cannot stop looking at. In it, my father stands in the garden of the house we grew up in, holding a sapling no taller than his knee. He is squinting into the sun. He is younger in the photograph than I am now, which still does not feel like something that should be possible.

The fig tree is enormous. I measured it last time I visited — the trunk is now wider than my arms can reach around. It produces more figs than any one family could reasonably eat, which means every August the whole neighborhood benefits, whether they know the source or not.

My father died three years ago. The things he left behind are strange and uneven in the way that all inheritances are. There is the watch I do not know how to wear without feeling like an impersonator. There is his handwriting in the margins of books I am still reading. And there is the tree, which does not grieve, does not remember, and does not need me to tend it. It simply keeps growing.

I find this both devastating and deeply comforting, which may be the same thing.

My daughter, who is seven, knows the tree as simply the tree. She does not know its history. She climbs it the way she climbs everything — without strategy, without fear, entirely present. Watching her, I think about how inheritance moves in directions we do not plan for.

He planted it the year I was born. She climbs it now. None of us asked for any of it. That is exactly the right way for love to work.

author.name: Yasmine Khalil
author.bio: Yasmine is a poet and essayist whose work explores grief, inheritance, and the persistence of the ordinary. Her collection was shortlisted for the Arab Writers Award.

title: Everything I Learned From Leaving
excerpt: The city I moved to was supposed to be temporary. Ten years later, I am still working out which version of home I am living in.
content:
When I moved to the new city, I told myself it was for two years. This is the fiction almost everyone tells themselves in the first weeks of a new place. You keep one foot emotionally at the door, just in case. You do not commit to a dentist. You describe everything in terms of what it is not quite as good as.

Somewhere around the fourteen-month mark, something shifted. I stopped comparing the coffee to coffee I had elsewhere. I started having a preferred table at a café I had at first described as fine. I noticed that I had begun giving directions to strangers — accurately, automatically, without consulting my phone.

Leaving teaches you things about yourself that staying cannot. It removes the scaffolding of your routine and asks you to find out which parts of you are structural and which were just habit. Some things I thought were essential turned out to be specific to a place. Some things I thought were optional turned out to be the main thing.

I missed the smell of my mother's apartment for a long time. Not her — I could call her, visit her — but the specific smell of that space, which is made up of things so layered and particular that they cannot be reconstructed anywhere else. This is what no one warns you about when you leave: the sensory residue of belonging.

I live here now. That is a sentence I had to practice saying before it felt true. I live here now, and this city has become one of the places I will one day be nostalgic for. Which means I am not leaving after all. I am just still arriving.

author.name: Rami Aziz
author.bio: Rami writes about displacement, belonging, and the strange loyalty we develop for places that were never supposed to be permanent.

title: Small Hours
excerpt: Depression does not always look like not getting out of bed. Sometimes it looks exactly like your regular life, just drained of color.
content:
For a long time I thought I was fine. This is the part nobody talks about — the years before you know, when the thing you are living with has no name yet and you mistake it for personality.

I was functional. I went to work. I met friends for dinner and laughed at the right moments. I was, by all external measures, doing well. But there was a glass between me and everything. Events arrived muffled. Happiness was always one remove from actual happiness — more like the memory of it, or the idea of it, recognizable but not quite real.

The doctor used the word dysthymia. I had to look it up afterward. A persistent low-level depression, running underneath everything like a piece of music you stop noticing because it has been playing too long. That description was so precise I read it three times.

What I want to say — the thing I wish someone had said to me — is that the absence of obvious darkness is not the same thing as light. You can be very tired and not look tired. You can be struggling and appear to everyone, including yourself, as someone who is simply a little quiet, a little reserved, someone who needs a lot of sleep.

I am better now. That sentence still surprises me a little, the way an unexpected view surprises you on a familiar walk. I did not know the color had been missing until it came back.

I tell this story not as a resolution but as a data point. In case someone reading it is living inside their own glass room and has not yet found the word for it. The word exists. So does what comes after.

author.name: Sara El-Masri
author.bio: Sara writes about mental health, identity, and recovery with the intention of making people feel less alone in difficult rooms. She lives and works in Cairo.
